headline: After Two Months in Captivity, 44 Nigerian Schoolchildren and Teachers Come Home
meta_description: Nigerian military rescues all 44 pupils and teachers abducted from three schools in Oyo state in May. Families celebrate their safe return.
summary_short: Forty-four pupils and teachers kidnapped from schools in Nigeria's Oyo state in May have been freed by the military after a month-long rescue operation.
body:
Two months of anguish have ended for families across Nigeria's Oyo state. The military announced Friday that all 44 pupils and teachers abducted from three schools on 15 May—Baptist Nursery and Primary School, LA Primary School, and Community Grammar School—have been rescued and several captors arrested.

The month-long operation, involving military, police, intelligence agencies and local vigilante groups, dismantled the abductors' network across the Old Oyo National Park forest. The military acknowledged that some soldiers died during the mission but did not provide details.

For families, the ordeal has been profound. Prof Wole Alamu, whose wife Rachael Folawe Alamu heads Community Grammar School, told the BBC that watching videos of her captivity released by abductors was especially difficult. "We thank God that it ended well," he said.

Hassan Ajibola, who leads the Teachers' Union in Oyo State, expressed "joy" at their return but stressed urgent action: schools need security personnel, CCTV, regular patrols, and reinforced fencing—measures outlined in the Safe School Initiative launched over a decade ago following the Chibok schoolgirls' abduction.

The freed captives are receiving medical treatment and will return home soon. The military says more operations are planned.
